text: The left lobe of the liver is separated from the Nathanson liver retractor, revealing the underlying structures. A Buzzi, thirty-six French instrument is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection of the short gastric vessels at the level of the second gastric pedicle, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully separate these vessels from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon is working on the short gastric vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the left-hand instrument to apply anteromedial traction to the anterior wall of the stomach, while the assistant holds the short gastric vessels with the right hand.

The surgeon uses a harmonic or ligature device to separate approximately twelve millimeters of the short gastric vessels towards the fundus, facilitating a floppy fundus for the wrap. The laparoscopic view shows the mobilization of the stomach and esophagus as part of the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon discusses the timing of dissecting the short gastric vessels, indicating that it is not mandatory to address the first short gastric vessel initially.

The surgeon begins by dissecting and mobilizing the esophagus, followed by the dissection of the short gastric vessels at the level of the second gastric pedicle, preparing for the Nissen fundoplication wrap.
The surgeon approaches the splenic hilum, visible just above the current dissection area, requiring careful maneuvering.
An assistant adjusts the position of the grasper to securely hold the short gastric vessels during the procedure.

The surgeon separates the posterior attachment of the short gastric vessels using surgical instruments, preparing the area for further mobilization of the stomach and esophagus during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, progressing towards the left crus of the diaphragm, carefully separating tissue layers.
The surgical team reaches the upper level of the dissection, progressing towards the splenic hilum.
The surgeon divides a section of the omentum, separating it from the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the stomach from the superior pole of the spleen at the level of the left crus, carefully separating visible adhesions between the stomach and spleen.
The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the stomach, facilitating further dissection and mobilization of the gastric structures.
The surgeon separates the adhesion between the stomach and the spleen, visibly detaching the two structures.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the stomach, finishing the adhesiolysis. Traction is applied to begin the anterior mobilization of the esophagus, with options to start from either the pars placida or the angular face.
The surgeon reaches the left crus, visually identifying it on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the stomach fundus, ensuring it is free from adhesions to the spleen and surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the pars flaccida and applies anterolateral traction to open the peritoneum over this anatomical area.
The surgeon identifies the caudate lobe of the liver, with the vena cava visible just below it.
The surgeon pulls the stomach downward to facilitate the separation of the anterior peritoneum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon applies traction to expose the esophagus, continuing the dissection in the region of the pars flaccida.
The anterior vagus nerve is visible embedded in the surrounding tissue near the caudate lobe and vena cava.
The surgeon stretches the stomach to prevent injury to the anterior vagus nerve during the procedure.

The surgeon addresses minor bleeding near the caudate lobe, ensuring not to extend posteriorly. Anterolateral traction is applied, and the anterior peritoneum over the esophagus is opened, revealing the diaphragmatic crura.
The surgeon separates the right grasp from the esophagus and proceeds to mobilize the posterior aspect of the esophagus, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to apply anterior traction to the oesophagus while a blunt grasper is used to open its jaws and create a window posterior to the oesophagus, moving from right to left.
The surgeon exposes the diaphragmatic crura, highlighting the anatomical structure during the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the right crus of the diaphragm during the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the oesophagus, which is exposed in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left crus of the diaphragm on the opposite side of the esophagus.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the left crus from the right side, creating a buttonhole posterior to the oesophagus.
The suction irrigation device is positioned on the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a grasping instrument on the left side, manipulating tissue near the left crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon performs posterior mobilization of the oesophagus, creating a posterior window by separating the tissue behind the oesophagus.
A posterior window is formed during the procedure, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon enlarges the posterior window created during the mobilization process, using surgical instruments to carefully expand the opening.
The surgeon prepares to take the gastric wrap, positioning it for the next step in the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon enlarges the posteriorly formed window using blunt dissection, creating sufficient space to proceed with the wrap during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon uses a grasper to take a bite for crural approximation, indicating the process of bringing the crura together.
The surgeon places a figure-of-eight mattress suture, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon takes a bite from the right crus using a surgical instrument, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon performs a reverse bite suture technique, manipulating the tissue with precision.
The surgeon performs an 'out to in' suture technique, likely continuing the process of crural approximation or wrap formation.
The surgeon performs a suture technique from inside to outside on the left crus, using a grasper to facilitate the stitch placement.
The surgeon takes another bite from the right crus using a surgical needle, continuing the suturing process.
The surgeon performs a suture from right to left, continuing the stitching technique during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon takes another suture bite, continuing the stitching process on the visible tissue.
The surgeon performs a suture from left to right, continuing the stitching process in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a mattress suture, which involves passing the needle through the tissue in a manner that creates a horizontal loop, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon places a figure-of-eight or mattress suture, securing tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the surgical site using an intracorporeal knot technique, securing the sutures within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon ties a Sargent's knot, demonstrating the knotting technique used in the procedure.
The surgeon performs two opposite alternating wraps around the esophagus, completing the fundoplication wrap configuration.
The surgeon demonstrates the crural approximation, ensuring it is not overly tight to allow for proper anatomical alignment.
The surgeon performs crural approximation, bringing the crura together using sutures to reinforce the esophageal hiatus.
The surgeon completes the crural approximation, securing the crura with sutures.
The surgeon trims the tail suture and removes the needle, then performs the fundus pull by grasping the fundus and pulling it out to create the fundus wrap.
The surgeon pulls the fundus wrap, visibly tightening it around the esophagus as part of the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon carefully pulls the posterior valve of the fundus wrap, ensuring that the esophagus remains untwisted during the maneuver.
The surgeon checks for the presence of the 'buttock sign,' ensuring that both sides of the fundus wrap appear symmetrical and evenly distributed.
The surgeon demonstrates the 'two buttock sign,' indicating symmetrical folds of the stomach fundus during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon completes the fixation of the fundoplication wrap, ensuring it is securely positioned around the esophagus without torsion.
The surgeon performs the final step of the Nissen fundoplication by taking the placation, securing the gastric fundus around the esophagus to complete the wrap.
The surgeon approximates the stomach to itself, visually aligning the gastric tissue as part of the fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon places two or three sutures to secure the fundoplication wrap, ensuring the stomach is properly approximated.
The surgeon performs a stomach-to-stomach wrap, visually approximating the stomach tissue layers to create the fundoplication.
The surgeon ties a tumble square knot or surgeon's knot to secure the stomach wrap, ensuring the sutures hold the stomach in place effectively.

The surgeon ties the first knot using a surgeon's knot technique, starting with a double wrap followed by two alternating single wraps. The knot is positioned, and a second knot is planned approximately two centimeters below.
The surgeon performs a stomach-to-stomach wrap, securing the stomach in position with sutures.
The surgeon holds the suture and forms a 'C' shape, adjusting the tail to be shorter.
The surgeon performs a double wrap knot on the suture, securing the stomach to stomach wrap.

The surgeon ties the second knot using a two-one-one technique, starting with a double wrap followed by two alternating single wraps. The knot is tightened securely, with the suture tail positioned to one side.
The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the needle after completing the knot tying process.
